skill.name: microsoft-graph-api
skill.description: Connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Microsoft Graph for Outlook email and calendar workflows through Bun TypeScript scripts.
skill.summary: Access Outlook mail and calendar data through Microsoft Graph scripts.
skill.author: BLTGV

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby or shell backtick execution, weak cryptography, C2 keywords, and path traversal are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code blocks, TypeScript template literals, and Microsoft Graph terminology. The real risks are legitimate but sensitive capabilities: OAuth token storage, Microsoft network access, mailbox read and send operations, and calendar write operations. No evidence found of malicious intent, data exfiltration to non-Microsoft endpoints, or prompt injection text.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext OAuth Credential Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill stores Microsoft Graph access and refresh tokens in a local JSON file under the user home directory. The write path does not set an explicit restrictive file mode, so token exposure depends on host defaults and filesystem permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly serializes OAuth credentials, including refresh tokens, to credentials.json. This is legitimate persistence, but the security impact is clear if the local file is readable by another process or user.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Microsoft Graph Permissions
medium_findings.description: The skill requests delegated permissions for reading mail, sending mail, reading calendars, and writing calendars. These permissions match the skill purpose, but compromise or misuse can expose mailbox content or modify calendar data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scope constants and command-specific scope selection are explicit. The risk is capability scope, not hidden behavior, because these permissions are required for the advertised features.

medium_findings.title: Email Sending Capability
medium_findings.description: The email script can send messages through /me/sendMail using user-provided recipients, subject, body, CC, and BCC fields. This is intended functionality, but it creates social, compliance, and accidental-send risk in agent workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The send command validates required fields and posts directly to the Graph sendMail endpoint. No evidence suggests malicious exfiltration, but the action is high impact and should require clear user approval.

medium_findings.title: Calendar Write Capability
medium_findings.description: The calendar script can create Microsoft Graph calendar events with subject, location, body, and attendees. This is expected behavior, but agent misuse can create unwanted events or invite external attendees.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The create command builds an event from command-line values and posts it to /me/events. The behavior is transparent and useful, but it modifies user data.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous static detections for Ruby or shell backtick execution come from Markdown command examples and TypeScript template literals. I did not find child_process usage, eval usage, or shell execution in the reviewed TypeScript code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced syntax is documentation fencing or JavaScript string interpolation. The reviewed files do not execute those strings through a shell.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography And C2 Keyword Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and C2 keyword detections appear to match benign words such as Graph, token, device code, and control-flow terms in OAuth code and documentation. No evidence found of custom cryptography, command-and-control behavior, or non-Microsoft exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is OAuth device-code authentication against Microsoft login endpoints. The code handles tokens but does not implement cryptographic primitives or contact suspicious infrastructure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Refresh Tokens On Disk
dangerous_patterns.description: Refresh tokens are stored in a local credentials file to support automatic token refresh. This enables convenience but increases impact if the user home directory or skill process is compromised.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential object includes accessToken and refreshToken fields and is written as JSON. This is direct evidence of persistent secret storage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Network Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts send bearer tokens to Microsoft Graph and Azure OAuth endpoints. The destinations are expected Microsoft services, but all network execution should be visible to users because mailbox and calendar data is sensitive.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are explicit fetch requests to Microsoft domains with OAuth data or Authorization headers. The endpoints align with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Triggered Data Modification
dangerous_patterns.description: The email and calendar scripts can change external state by sending email and creating calendar events. These operations should be gated by explicit user confirmation before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code posts to /me/sendMail and /me/events. These are clear state-changing operations that can affect other people.

content.user_title: Manage Microsoft Graph Email and Calendar
content.value_statement: Microsoft 365 email and calendar tasks often require switching tools and copying context. This skill lets Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use Microsoft Graph scripts to read, search, send, and schedule with user authorization.
actual_capabilities: Lists Outlook messages from a selected mail folder with sender, subject, preview, read status, and attachment status.
Reads a specific Outlook message by Microsoft Graph message ID, including body content and metadata.
Searches Outlook messages and lists mail folders through Microsoft Graph endpoints.
Sends Outlook email with To, CC, BCC, subject, text body, or HTML body options.
Lists, views, searches, and creates Microsoft calendar events with attendees, location, body, and all-day options.
Handles Microsoft device-code authentication, token refresh, and multiple local credential profiles.
limitations: Requires Bun, Microsoft Graph access, and an authorized Microsoft account before data can be retrieved.
Stores OAuth credentials locally in a JSON file instead of using an encrypted keychain.
Does not implement automatic pagination or retry handling for Microsoft Graph rate limits.
Does not validate recipient domains, event attendee domains, or outgoing message content policy.

use_cases.title: Review Inbox Priorities
use_cases.description: List unread or recent Outlook messages, inspect selected messages, and prepare a concise priority summary.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Meetings
use_cases.description: Check upcoming Microsoft calendar events, search for related meetings, and create follow-up events with attendees.

use_cases.title: Automate Microsoft 365 Workflows
use_cases.description: Use Graph-backed scripts from an AI coding environment to test email and calendar automation flows.

prompt_templates.title: List Recent Email
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my 10 most recent Outlook emails from the inbox and summarize which ones need a response.

prompt_templates.title: Find A Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Search my Outlook mail for messages from finance about the quarterly report, then summarize the matching subjects and dates.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Meeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my calendar for tomorrow, then draft a one-hour follow-up meeting with the project team. Ask before creating it.

prompt_templates.title: Send Approved Follow-Up
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a follow-up email to the listed attendees from today’s review meeting. Show me the recipients, subject, and body before sending.

output_examples.input: Show my upcoming calendar events for this week.
output_examples.output: A concise agenda with event titles, times, organizers, locations, and attendee response status where available.

output_examples.input: Search for unread messages about contract review.
output_examples.output: A short list of matching messages with sender, subject, received date, and preview text for quick triage.

output_examples.input: Create a meeting for tomorrow with the design team.
output_examples.output: A confirmation-ready meeting summary with title, start time, end time, attendees, location, and description before creation.

best_practices: Ask for explicit user approval before sending email or creating calendar events.
Use the narrowest command and profile needed for the current task.
Delete unused credential profiles and protect the local credentials file.
anti_patterns: Do not send email based only on inferred intent or incomplete recipient details.
Do not request broad mailbox searches when a folder, sender, or date range is available.
Do not expose raw access tokens, refresh tokens, or device codes in chat output.

faq.question: What Microsoft services does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses Microsoft Graph for Outlook mail and Microsoft calendar operations through OAuth-authenticated requests.

faq.question: Does it require user authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It starts Microsoft device-code authentication and stores authorized credentials for later use.

faq.question: Can it send email?
faq.answer: Yes. It can send Outlook email when the Mail.Send permission is authorized and the user approves the action.

faq.question: Can it create calendar events?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create Microsoft calendar events with attendees, location, body text, and all-day settings.

faq.question: Where are credentials stored?
faq.answer: Credentials are stored in a local JSON file under the user home configuration directory.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings because the behavior is transparent, but it handles sensitive Microsoft 365 data.
